_comment: VISUAL AUDIT — the Cleanup & unify findings, in designer language. Each finding shows the real variations you have today, rendered LIVE from their measured values (no screenshots to go stale), where each one appears, exactly what differs, and a Keep / Merge / Discuss recommendation. Measured at 1280px across 25 real pages. Prototype-only scaffolding (#devStates, .live-sim) is excluded — it never ships. Specimens stamp their own rendered height/size, so a label can never drift from what you actually see.
_specimen: spec = { tag, text, stage:'light'|'dark', css:{…} } → rendered inline with those exact values.

findings.title: The small pill button — two heights, side by side
findings.question: Your nav's two buttons sit next to each other but are not the same height. Should they be?
differs: Height: a 5px gap between two buttons that sit side by side and should match
Everything else is identical: same padding, same 14px type, same pill radius, same border
findings.cause: Not a design decision. "Log in" is written as a <button>, "Get matched" as a link. Browsers give buttons a tighter default line-height, so the same button class comes out two heights depending on which tag it happens to be written as.
findings.reasoning: There is no design intent here — nobody chose to make them different. The two buttons are meant to be a matched pair in the nav, and today they visibly aren't, on 18 pages. Because the cause is line-height inheritance rather than a style choice, this same bug is what splits your big CTA button into three heights further down. Fixing it once, at the button, fixes the whole family.
findings.fix: Add line-height: 1 to .btn so height comes from padding only, never from the surrounding page or the tag.

variants.label: Filled — written as a link
variants.note: The nav "Get matched" — visibly taller than the Log in beside it

variants.label: Outlined — written as a link
variants.note: Same class as the nav Log in, but taller here — because it's written as a link

after.label: One height — set by padding, not by the tag
Matches, whatever tag it's written as

findings.impact_note: Fixes a visible mismatch in the nav on 18 pages, and collapses the big-button group as a side effect. One line of CSS.

findings.title: The big pill button — six different heights
findings.question: You have six large pill buttons. Do you need more than one size of "big button"?
differs: Height: six values spanning 16px, from the smallest to the largest
Type size: 15px or 16px; weight 600 or 700
findings.cause: Partly the same line-height bug as the nav pair (two of these are identical apart from being a link vs a button; another two are the same class rendering differently on different pages). Partly genuinely different padding chosen page by page.
findings.reasoning: A visitor cannot tell these apart — the middle differences carry no meaning, they're accidents of where each button was built. But the jump from the smallest to the largest IS meaningful: a compact button in a search bar should be smaller than the closing call-to-action. So don't collapse to one — collapse to a deliberate scale of two, and delete the rest.
findings.fix: Apply the small-pill fix first (it merges the 57/59/61 group on its own), then decide by hand whether you keep one big size or two.

variants.label: The same CTA class, on About
variants.note: Same class as the one before it — two heights

variants.label: Pricing card
variants.note: Sits directly above a shorter button in the same pricing row

findings.impact_note: Six large buttons become two deliberate sizes. Removes 4 one-off button looks from the system.

findings.title: The little green label — five different versions
findings.question: All five say the same thing visually. Should they be one?
differs: Letter-spacing: 1.54, 1.1, 1.68, 1.44px — four different tracking values
Size: 11px or 12px
Weight: 700 or 800
All are plain green uppercase — the pill rule is holding everywhere
findings.cause: Each page that needed a green label got its own version instead of reusing the one that exists.
findings.reasoning: Nobody can see the difference between 1.44 and 1.54 letter-spacing — but the system pays for all five forever, and every new page picks one at random. Two of these (the Coming-soon and Book-a-call labels) are exactly the invented eyebrows your own rules ban. Merging costs you nothing visually and closes the door on a sixth.
findings.fix: Merge the two invented eyebrows (Coming-soon, Book-a-call) into .eyebrow and delete their rules. The category tag and benefit-card label carry layout of their own — align those tracking values by hand.

variants.label: The Coming-soon label
variants.note: An invented eyebrow — the rules ban this

variants.label: The Book-a-call label
variants.note: An invented eyebrow — the rules ban this

findings.impact_note: Five green labels become one. Closes the door on a sixth, and removes two eyebrows your own rules ban.

findings.title: The muted tag pill — four near-identical versions
findings.question: Four grey pills that all label a topic or skill. Is any difference meaningful?
differs: Size: 11, 12, 13px
Fill: soft off-white on two, transparent on two
Border: two have a hairline, two don't
Padding differs by 1–3px on each
findings.cause: Each illustration was built with its own tag rather than sharing one.
findings.reasoning: These do exactly the same job — labelling a skill, a domain, a requirement — and a visitor reads them as the same thing. The variations aren't communicating a difference in meaning; they're four people solving the same problem on four different days. One tag, one look.
findings.fix: One tag: 12px / 500 / soft off-white fill / pill. Retire the three others.
findings.impact_note: Four tag styles become one. Every future tag has an obvious answer instead of a coin flip.

findings.title: The status badge — three sizes
findings.question: These flag status ("Top match", "New match", "Best value"). Should they be one size?
differs: Size: 10px, 11px, 12px
One is uppercase, two are sentence case
One is a solid green fill, two are tinted green
findings.reasoning: The tinted-green pair are clearly the same thing at two sizes — merge those. But the solid-green "Best value" is doing a louder job: it's a sales flag on a pricing card, not a status. That one may deserve to stay different — that's a call about emphasis, not consistency, and it's yours to make.
findings.fix: Merge the two tinted badges to 11px / 600. Decide separately whether the solid "Best value" flag stays its own thing.

variants.label: Solid — 12px
variants.note: A sales flag, not a status

findings.impact_note: Two of three badges merge; the third is a real decision about emphasis.

findings.title: Card corners — five different roundings
findings.question: Your scale says cards are 20px and feature images are 28px. You're using five values. Do the other three earn their place?
differs: 16px, 18px, 20px, 22px, 28px
16, 18 and 22 are not on your scale at all
findings.cause: The system is right and the pages disagree with it: ptf.css already says cards are 20px (var(--r-xl)). The 22px comes from pages overriding it locally — employers/index.html and talents/index.html each re-declare border-radius: 22px on the testimonial card. 16px and 18px are likewise set on the page, not in the system.
findings.reasoning: Corner radius is one of the strongest signals that things belong to the same family. At these sizes 18 vs 20 vs 22 is invisible one at a time — but side by side it quietly reads as sloppiness, and every new card becomes a guess. What makes this worth fixing: your system already has the right answer. These are pages ignoring it, which is the pattern that will keep reappearing until the overrides are gone.
findings.fix: Set every card to the system's 20px. Eight rules across Employers, Hire and Talents (v2) — the 28px feature panels (Sign in, Book a call) stay as they are, because 28px is the second tier this finding recommends, not a mistake.

variants.label: 22px
variants.note: The testimonial card — off-scale, 2px from the standard

findings.impact_note: Three off-scale radii disappear and cards read as one family. Removes page overrides that fight the system.

findings.title: Card shadows — twelve recipes for one effect
findings.question: You have one shadow token. Twelve different shadows are actually on screen. Do you want more than two?
differs: Blur ranges from 4px to 80px
Opacity ranges from 4% to 18%
Some are single-layer, some are two-layer
Only one of the twelve is your token
findings.cause: Each floating element got a hand-tuned shadow.
findings.reasoning: Shadow is how the eye reads depth — inconsistent shadows make things sit at different heights for no reason, which is exactly the "accidental" look you're trying to remove. You realistically need two: a resting card, and something genuinely lifted (a floating card, a modal). Everything else is noise.
findings.fix: Two elevations: Resting (your existing token) and Lifted. Retire the other ten.
findings.impact_note: Twelve shadows become two. Depth starts meaning something.

findings.title: The hero headline — the same style at two sizes and two weights
findings.question: Your big page headline comes out 68px on some pages and 80px on others. Intentional?
differs: Size: 68px vs 80px — a 12px jump
Weight: 700 vs 800
Both use the same headline style name
findings.cause: Individual pages override the size and weight locally, so the shared headline doesn't actually control it.
findings.reasoning: A hero deliberately sized bigger on a flagship page is a legitimate design decision — but right now it isn't a decision, it's whatever each page did. And the weight difference (700 vs 800) at the same 80px is almost certainly accidental: two pages showing the same headline in different thicknesses. Decide if you want one hero size or a deliberate two, then make the system say so.
findings.fix: The odd one out is a second rule for the same headline: .hero h1 at talents/index-v2.html:309 (80px, Extra bold) sits below h1.hero-headline at line 18 (80px, Bold) and wins. Either answer starts by fixing that.
findings.impact_note: One hero size instead of whatever each page chose. Also settles a 700-vs-800 weight split.

options.label: One weight — Bold everywhere
options.note: Fixes the single odd rule. Keeps 68px and 80px as they are.

options.label: One weight and one size — 80px Bold
options.note: Every hero becomes the big one. Three pages grow from 68px.

findings.title: Event filters — two looks on the same page
findings.question: The Events page filters "Upcoming" with an underline list and "Past" with pill chips. Should one page have two filter styles?
differs: A left-aligned list with a green/ink left bar vs a horizontal row of bordered pills
Active state: bold ink text vs a solid ink fill
31px vs 35px tall
findings.cause: The archive list uses a grid variant that restyles the filter; the upcoming list uses the sidebar style.
findings.reasoning: This one is genuinely arguable, so I'm not calling it. The layouts differ (a sidebar beside a list vs filters above a grid), and a sidebar filter reasonably looks different from a toolbar filter. But they're the same control doing the same job, twice, on one screen — and a visitor scrolling past may read them as two different mechanisms. Worth a decision either way.
findings.fix: If you want one: use the pill chips in both places. If you want two, say so in the system so it's a decision, not a leftover.

spec.html: Show allVirtual job fairsSummits
Show allVirtual job fairsSummits

findings.impact_note: One filter control instead of two on the same screen — or a documented reason for both.

findings.title: A secondary button on dark that exists once
findings.question: About has a faint outlined button on dark that appears nowhere else. Keep it?
differs: A thin grey outline on dark, vs the white filled button beside it
It's the only outlined-on-dark button in the whole site
findings.cause: About needed a second, quieter action and one was invented for it.
findings.reasoning: The need is real — a dark closing band with two actions needs a loud one and a quiet one, and today the system only gives you the loud one. So this isn't drift to delete; it's a gap to fill properly. Either promote it to the system as the official secondary-on-dark, or drop the second action on About.
findings.fix: Promote it as a proper secondary-on-dark button, or remove it.
findings.impact_note: Either fills a real gap in the system (secondary action on dark) or removes a one-off.

findings.title: A default browser-blue link
findings.question: This isn't a design decision — it's an unstyled link. Fix?
differs: Browser default blue, underlined — a colour that appears nowhere in your palette
findings.cause: A plain email link inside a FAQ answer with no styling applied.
findings.reasoning: Nothing to weigh — this is a miss, not a variation. It's the only browser-blue on the site.
findings.fix: Style links inside FAQ answers with your green link colour.

variants.label: Today — in the Virtual job fair FAQ

spec.html: If your question wasn't answered here, email the team at hi@powertofly.com.
If your question wasn't answered here, email the team at hi@powertofly.com.

findings.impact_note: Removes the only browser-blue on the site.

findings.title: The same “coming soon” page, built four times
findings.question: Four pages share one identical layout that the system has never named. Should it become a Section Pattern?
differs: Nothing — the four are structurally identical: eyebrow, big headline, sub, CTA row
It exists in ptf.css but appears nowhere in your component inventory
So the fifth coming-soon page will be rebuilt by hand, not reused
findings.cause: It was built once for a placeholder page and copied three more times. It works, so nobody promoted it into the system — which means it's a pattern in practice but not on paper.
findings.impact_note: Names a layout you've already reused four times. The next placeholder page becomes copy-and-change instead of build-from-scratch.
findings.reasoning: This is the opposite of drift — four pages agreeing perfectly is a pattern proving itself. The problem is only that it's undocumented, so it can't be found or reused deliberately, and each copy is free to wander. Reuse beats adapt beats create: this one is already at reuse, it just needs a name. Promote it to a Section Pattern in the inventory and it stops being an accident.
findings.fix: Document it in ds/COMPONENT-INVENTORY.md as the Coming-soon section pattern, and add it to the storybook so it can be found.

variants.label: Used identically on all four pages

spec.html:
Coming soon
Why PowerToFly
We're putting this page together. In the meantime, explore the rest of the playbook.
Book a discovery call

findings.title: Three event pages are one recipe wearing three hats
findings.question: Virtual job fair, Summit and Chat & Learn share 44 classes and the same skeleton. Should that become a Page Recipe?
differs: The three pages share 44 classes and the same section order
Hero, facts, people, how-it-works, event list, FAQ — same skeleton every time
Only the copy, the event type and the imagery change
findings.cause: They were built as a family on purpose — which is why they agree. But the family only exists in your head and in three HTML files, not in the system.
findings.impact_note: Turns three hand-built siblings into one recipe. The fourth event type becomes an afternoon instead of a rebuild.
findings.reasoning: You already made the hard call — these three agreeing is a real design decision, not an accident. The risk is what happens next: the fourth event page gets built from whichever sibling someone copies, and the family quietly splits. Writing it down as a Page Recipe — this hero, then these sections, in this order — locks in the work you've already done and makes the next one reuse instead of re-derive.
findings.fix: Document the event-detail Page Recipe (section order + which components fill each slot) and point /new-page at it.

spec.html:
Event hero
Facts
Speakers
How it works
More events
FAQ
CTA

findings.title: Components your pages use that the system never wrote down
findings.question: These are in ptf.css and on real pages, but in no inventory. Adopt them, or remove them?
differs: The event thumbnail, people row and faces strip: 4 pages
The about-stat card: 3 pages
None appear in ds/COMPONENT-INVENTORY.md
So the contract says they don't exist — while the pages use them daily
findings.cause: They were added straight to ptf.css while building the event pages, and the inventory never caught up.
findings.impact_note: Closes the gap between what the contract says exists and what your pages actually use — the gap that makes the contract ignorable.
findings.reasoning: Your build contract says: if it isn't in the inventory, it doesn't exist. These are used on up to four pages each, so right now the contract is quietly false — and a rule that's visibly wrong stops being followed. Either they've earned their place and should be documented, or they're one-offs to fold into existing components. Both are fine; leaving them undocumented isn't.
findings.fix: Decide per component: document it in the inventory + add a story, or fold it into an existing component.

baseline._comment: What this audit was measured against. The panel compares this to the live scan to tell you when the findings have gone stale. It never invents findings from a diff.
